---
title: Lighting up Five Cats
author: Jeffrey S. Racine
date: 2023-11-18
draft: false
feed: true
---

Well, when life gives you lemons, you don't have make lemonade do you?

This is to document my recent experiences with the five cats, or more accurately Ethernet Category Five (Cat 5) cables, fiber optic internet connections, and related things.

Some background is in order. Over a decade ago we bundled many home utilities (cellular phone, land line, internet, television) into a single package with a single provider (we then had 100 MB download speeds, 20 MB upload), Bell Canada. But my workflow involves shuffling large amounts of data back and forth from home to the university which taxed the system, and although Bell kept saying we would see fiber optic line in our neighborhood for years, it never materialized. Then a competitor (Cogeco) provided much faster service to our neighborhood via coaxial cabling (1 GB download, 30 MB upload) and I switched, and was fairly happy. But we had line issues over the years (corrosion) though they were always resolved. A few years ago Bell finally brought fiber to our neighborhood, but I was not compelled to switch, and Cogeco always matched the Bell prices (which were less expensive) so that kept me happy. However, in early October the speeds dropped (upload in particular) and the service became unacceptable, as with 15 MB upload speeds using rsync to synchronize home and work computers was taking so long it was interfering with my workflow. So naturally I contacted Cogego, and a total shitshow erupted with 5 visits from technicians who never came with the right equipment, or were not given any instructions or details about previous visits, and after the 5th visit where the tech said "you have a signal problem" (diagnosed by the first tech after his second visit on the same day as he arrived with a broken signal meter) I started thinking about switching.

But fate was being finicky. The first Cogeco rep said they offer newer Wi-Fi (mesh Wi-Fi 6) and on the 3rd visit they switched out our equipment (the old equipment was a router and two *wired* extenders that serviced the entire house fairly well). They installed the new router and two mesh *pods*, and then the show began. We had much worse network coverage throughout the house, and they recommended getting additional pods which became 6 in total. But still things were not good, and I started digging around for information on the system (Sagemcom Fast 3896 and Plume Superpods) and realized that I might need to hard wire the pods to the router. So, to experiment I bought two 100 foot Cat 6 Ethernet and started to experiment.

![Experimenting with Cat 6 Ethernet cabling - The Cogeco Mesh Shitshow](images/3F1230BD-D508-4FE0-B547-994D56544834.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}

But the happy intervention of fate occurred while I was reading up on mesh networks and read that to get the best speeds from my computer I should connect my computer directly to the router, if at all possible ("backhaul" is a term I became familiar with and enamored of). So I experimented with a direct connection, and the speeds were amazing (1 GB download, but sadly still 15 MB upload due to the ongoing signal issue). I also learned that these routers have 1 10 GB/s port, the rest 1 GB/s. Cogeco's shitshow turned into a learning experience in many ways. I discovered a utility called *networkQuality* that measures realized network speed, and was getting around 800 MB/s down and 15 MB/s up. You may think "this guy is an idiot, everyone knows they should do this", but I didn't, and I am not alone.

Let me also point out a totally unexpected positive outcome from the Cogeco shitshow. After running *networkQuality* at home, I tried it at work, and was surprised to say the least. My office at the university has 1 GB/s Ethernet, but I was only seeing 80 MB/s up and down according to networkQuality? WTF?

I contacted UTS and they tested the line and confirmed, but suggested I first try a different patch cord, which I did, and the culprit was a mis-installed Cat 5 Ethernet cable that apparently has a maximum transmission speed of 100 MB/s.

My email to University Technology Services (UTS), for posterity:

> Greetings, 
>
> I am noticing that the network speed in my office is quite slow, and transferring some larger files takes longer than I would expect (I expect around 1 gbs for a hard wired connection, hence my enquiry).
>
> Running networkQuality (macOS Sonoma, new iMac with Apple Silicon, with static IP/ethernet) reveals, consistently, around 85 mbps up and down... here is a recent summary...
>
> ==== SUMMARY ====\
> Uplink capacity: 83.883 Mbps\
> Downlink capacity: 84.392 Mbps\
> Responsiveness: Medium (186.335 milliseconds \| 322 RPM)\
> Idle Latency: 12.125 milliseconds \| 5000 RPM
>
> So, my question is whether this is somehow compromised or not? In other words, why is the connection so slow, can what can be done to increase the speed? On my home connection I get 10x these speeds with a plain vanilla setup, and would expect it to be if anything faster on campus.
>
> Thanks!

Their response, for posterity:

> Hi Jeff, the connection for **130.113.139.86** is connecting on our switch at 100 Mbps instead of 1Gbps, assuming the device is capable of that. I tried a port reset but it's still 100 Mbps. Sometimes a bad patch cord can cause this. Are you able to swap the patch cord, or swap it with another system to see if the problem follows?
>
> If that doesn't work I will have the line tested.
>
> **130.113.139.87** is connecting at 1Gbps.

I tried a Cat 6 cable and voila! 1 GB/s up and down! So, I had been living for years with 1/10th of the speed I was supposed to be getting, who knew? Thanks Cogeco! Without your shitshow I would have remained ignorant, so gratitude is in order.

Then I realized I needed to cut the cord with Cogeco and switch as they were totally unresponsive. Bell recently installed fiber in our neighborhood, and I called and found out I could get 3 GB up and down for the same price I was paying Cogeco for 1 GB up and 30 MB down. Decision made tossing and turning early Saturday morning after the Friday evening visit from the Cogeco tech who confirmed "you have a signal problem". After a bit of a frustrating experience with Bell[^1] I ordered their service and waited for the install (yesterday, 6 days after deciding to switch). I was nervous as I didn't know whether they might have to dig up our front yard or not, whether they could move the router from the basement to a more central location, whether they could hard wire my computer to enable the full 3 GB/s experience, whether they could hard wire a pod to reach the far side of the house, etc.) and they said the tech would arrive between 8-10 AM and that it would take between 4-6 hours (it took 6+ with my help). But the Bell tech was a super understanding and very competent professional. He texted me prior to arriving to give me a heads up, arrived, inspected and said "I need to go off-site to turn on the lights", and returned 20 minutes later and got to work.

[^1]: I desperately wanted a Bell representative to first inspect our property and let us know if there would be any installation issues before I ordered, but they don't allow that which is unfortunate as I was willing to pay out-of-pocket for someone to do so.

![Bell Arrives On Time and Equipped](images/B022A8E1-7A34-41B3-9135-C013DD97F86E.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}

He next installed fiber from the curb to the house, drilled a hole to pass the cable into the house, and then the two of us moved the new router to the middle of the house on the main floor and threaded the fiber directly to the new location of the router (the old router was situated on one side of the house in the basement where the coaxial cable entered the house).

![Fiber Is In The House](images/D7F19534-D431-4194-A859-3F2FEFD87F6D.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}

![Let There Be Light](images/A80B1AA5-EDCC-4DB2-8EFF-EDCEB1D3B1B7.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}

![Fusion Happening Indoors](images/49FACC5A-83C7-40C4-9445-1401B06C482E.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}

After, installed Ethernet cables to my computer and a pod on the far side of the house, and though the tech had 6 pods we kept only 4 and he felt this was overkill (he was right). When we first fired up my computer to test the speed, it was only 1 GB/s and I suspected a cable issue, but he had not plugged the cable into the 10 GB port and once he did we got 3.1 GB up and down, and he was positively giddy and took a screenshot declaring it was the fastest he has ever seen (typically maxes out around 2.5 he said). I had some issues with interference, and the Bell Wi-Fi app won't be available to me for a week as this is a new install so I can't yet optimize the system, but all is good. And, as an aside, the Bell Fibe TV is much better than Cogeco's, and the Bell Fibe app is amazing (I can watch TV on my phone or tablet anywhere in the house, and it is super fast and responsive). So better internet, better TV, no increase in price and the very latest Wi-Fi 6E equipment, so color me happy).


![After The (shit) Show](images/CF7BBA67-8D3E-45BF-98F9-4A8EDABD9FDD.jpg){fig-align="center" width="200"}
